titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 17 September 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I heard not of this messengers being here, nor of his intended jorny to Ostend till he was almost going; and though I had known yt sooner, yet could I not have writ you much more then I am like to do on this sodain: for here growes no newes at Knebworth,The Hertfordshire house of Rowland Lytton. unles I shold tell you how well harvest is come home, or some other adventures of hawking, hunting, bowlinge or such like. But what I want of mine owne store I will borrow of my neighbours, and for brevities sake in this haste send you a letter I receved fowre or five dayes since that shewes how the world went at the writing thereof, whereto I can only adde that this day sevenight the erle of Essex went to the court but with what successe, or how matters stand since, I know not.Essex took his place in the Council on September 10. (A.P.C., xxix, 154–157.)

The Quene removed on Wensday toward Nonsuch taking Dr. CesarsJulius Caesar (knighted 1603), Master of Requests in Ordinary. On September 12 Queen Elizabeth visited him at Mitcham, Surrey, spent the night there, and dined with him the next day. in her way who had provided for her eight severall times. Sir John BrocketSir John Brockett of Brockett Hall, Hertfordshire. His wife was Ellen Lytton, a relative of Rowland Lytton. His daughter Elizabeth was the first wife of George Carleton and the mother of John Carleton, the nephew here mentioned. lies languishing and is thought will not hold out long, but whether be better for your nephew that he go or tarry is a question.

On Thursday your cousin Lytton and I receved your letters of the 29 of August and the 8th of this present, which came so fully furnished with ballads, bookes and babies that we cannot overcome them yet, specially Aldegondes tedious discourse.Philip van Marnix van Sint Aldegonde was the author of several religious discourses that were in print before 1598. Yf he were now at home I know he wold send you many thancks, which I wold faine supplie yf I were as goode at yt as himself, but you know my plain dealinge and therefore I hope will expect no ceremonies. He went yesterday to BletsoBletsoe, Bedfordshire, the seat of Oliver, third Lord St. John. to convoy the Lords, and Lady PelhamJudith, daughter of Oliver, first Lord St. John, and widow of Sir John Pelham of Sandhurst, Sussex. that hath lien here this fortnight, and wold faine have drawne me along with him, but by goode hap a certain cold and hoarsenes kept me at home, for how well so ever they use me, yet me thincks I am out of my element when I am among Lords, and I am of Rabelais minde that they looke big comme un millord d'Angleterre.

I can send you nothing of your privat frends neither can I tell certainly when I shalbe at London, but toward the terme have with you to Westminster, till when I bid you farewell with my best and kindest wishes.

postscript.p: To my assured goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.

div.head: Editor's Note
div.p: Written hastily from Knebworth, the Hertfordshire seat of Rowland Lytton, this brief letter is notable for what it withholds as much as what it reports. Chamberlain, caught off guard by the messenger's imminent departure, confesses he has no news of his own and must borrow a recently received letter to send along. The one piece of court intelligence he can confirm is that Essex returned to the Privy Council on September 10—though Chamberlain himself does not know the outcome. A brief mention of Sir John Brockett's failing health raises the question of what will happen to Carleton's nephew (Brockett's grandson John Carleton), a thread that will recur in later letters. The letter's most personal passage describes Rowland Lytton escorting Lady Pelham—Judith St. John, a widow long connected to the household—to Bletsoe, seat of Oliver, third Lord St. John, while Chamberlain stays behind with a cold. His self-deprecating Rabelais quotation—"they look grand like an English lord"—reflects his characteristic ambivalence toward aristocratic company.
